content:
The proposal is accepted. The milestone is set. And the name signed at the bottom of it is the part nobody in the comments wants to talk about.

7 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

A few days ago I asked r/golang a question I had been sitting on for months, half hoping someone would talk me out of it: has Go started moving away from its original philosophy?
A hundred and thirty-nine upvotes and sixty comments later, I had been called nostalgic, thanked, corrected, lectured, and diagnosed with PTSD from other languages’ type systems. Some of it stung. Some of it was fair. I argued back more than I should have.
But halfway down that thread, someone dropped a link, almost casually, the way you mention the weather. Issue 77273 on the Go repository. Generic methods for Go. Label: Proposal-Accepted. Milestone: Go 1.27.
I read the proposal twice that night. It is careful, well-reasoned, and fully backward-compatible. It is also, I think, the clearest signal yet that the language Rob Pike designed in 2007 and the language shipping next release are two different projects that happen to share a name.
